presentation_title: Analysis of the Initial Psychometric Performance of the Fundamentals of Endoscopic Surgery (FES) Skills Examination
presentation_scope: Research and validation study of FES manual skills exam, not a performance task video

frame_analyses.content: FES task equipment and simulator overview
Research paper citation - Vassilou et al., validation study (111 test takers analyzed)
Mueller et al. study - portable platform validity evidence (r=0.86, p<0.001)
Mueller et al. colonoscopy performance correlation (r=0.8 with GAGES-C score)
Flexible endoscopy curriculum - FES certification requirements
Goal statement - validate FES exam with increased test taker volume
Methods - 344 FES examinees, first attempt; PGY2 through 20+ years experience; 77% male
Measures - five endoscopic tasks (Navigation, Loop Reduction, Retroflexion, Mucosal Inspection, Tool Targeting); demographics
Score distributions across five FES endoscopic tasks; 81% overall pass rate; Loop Reduction lowest median
Task-specific vs. general ability - correlations small to moderate (r=.08-.48); item-total correlations .27-.54
Scores by experience - little relationship to training level; total endoscopy case volume positively related (8-point jump 1-150 vs. 151-300+ cases)
Scores by experience continued - lower endoscopy case volume predicts most tasks; upper endoscopy case volume predicts Loop Reduction
Other variables - no difference between simulator types (Mentor II vs. Express); glove size, height, gender all related; markedly lower pass rates for glove size 6 (59% vs. 83-85%)
Conclusions - 5 tasks assess similar but distinct skills; Loop Reduction and Mucosal Inspection most task-specific; Loop Reduction most difficult
Future considerations - non-compensatory scoring, closer evaluation of Loop Reduction task, monitor validity threats over time

scoring_applicability: NOT APPLICABLE - This is a research presentation, not a performance video
why_not_scoreable: This video contains PowerPoint slides presenting research findings on FES exam validity and reliability. It is not a video of a trainee or surgeon performing an FES task. No performance data, timing, or technical execution is visible. The presentation discusses the *design* and *outcomes* of an FES validation study but does not show actual task performance.

teaching_content.research_focus: Psychometric validation of FES manual skills examination
key_findings: 111-344 test takers analyzed; 81% overall pass rate
Strong correlation with clinical colonoscopy performance (r=0.73-0.86)
Loop Reduction and Mucosal Inspection are most task-specific skills
Total endoscopy case volume predicts FES performance more than training level
Glove size 6 associated with significantly lower pass rates (59% vs. 83-85%)
No difference between simulator platforms (Mentor II vs. Express)
Gender and physical anthropometry (height, glove size) significantly related to performance
validity_evidence: Internal structure: Small to moderate inter-task correlations (r=.08-.48), suggesting distinct skill constructs
Relationship to clinical variables: Colonoscopy performance correlates with FES scores (r=0.8)
Construct-related evidence: Case volume more predictive than training level
Threats to validity: Potential construct-irrelevant variance from glove size and physical factors
clinical_implications: FES exam demonstrates validity for assessing foundational endoscopic skills
Case volume/experience is better predictor of competency than tenure level
Equipment standardization (simulator type) does not significantly impact scores
Physical factors (glove size) may create barriers independent of skill competency
implications_for_fls_assessment: Loop Reduction identified as most challenging and most task-specific task
FES tasks measure a mix of general and task-specific abilities
Recommendation for future non-compensatory scoring to address construct validity

confidence_rationale: Video classification is certain - this is unambiguously a conference presentation slide deck with research findings, not a performance video. No FLS task is being executed.
